text: robotic subtotal colectomy for synchronous colon cancers, and pan colitis.

This case involves a twenty-five-year-old woman who presented with a history of three months of worsening lower abdominal pain, both in the right and left lower abdomen, thirty pounds of weight loss, anorexia, episodes of watery diarrhea, and intermittent bouts of emesis. There was no other significant medical or surgical history.
Prior workup at an outside hospital included right upper quadrant ultrasound, HIDA scan, plain abdominal x-rays, and upper endoscopy, all of which were negative.
Her symptoms persisted and she developed anemia with a hemoglobin of eight point eight. This prompted a CAT scan of the abdomen and pelvis.

Imaging revealed diffuse wall thickening of the terminal ilium and cecum, edema of the right and transverse colon, findings suggestive of a mass in the right colon, and mesenteric adenopathy in the right lower quadrant with lymph nodes as large as one point four centimeters.
Colonoscopy was performed, confirming a friable mass in the cecum, causing near obstruction, as well as severe colitis of the right colon through the distal transverse colon.

Biopsies revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right colon mass, as well as high-grade dysplasia in the distal right colon, separate from the mass, a sessile serrated adenoma in the distal right colon, low-grade dysplasia in the transverse colon, and normal mucosa in the left sigmoid and rectum.

A CEA level at this time was one point eight. Because of the right colon cancer, the documentation of high-grade dysplasia and low-grade dysplasia in the right and transverse colon, and the patient's age, robotic-assisted subtotal colectomy was recommended by consensus of a multi-specialty tumor committee.
The surgical procedure is conducted using the da Vinci SI robotic system, involving two separate dockings for optimal access and maneuverability.

The surgical team places a camera in the inframammary position, followed by the insertion of an 8mm port in the left upper quadrant, an 8mm port in the right lower quadrant at the planned incision site, and a 5mm assist port in the left lower quadrant.
During laparoscopic exploration, the surgeon observes local invasion of the right colon mass into the abdominal wall, with no visible signs of carcinomatosis or metastatic disease.
The patient is positioned left side down, and the robotic patient cart is docked over the right upper abdomen, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection using monopolar scissors to excise normal-appearing abdominal wall tissue en bloc with the specimen, aiming to achieve a grossly negative radial margin.
The surgeon uses a monopolar scissor for sharp dissection and a fenestrated bipolar instrument for blunt retraction, achieving adequate exposure by utilizing the side of the instrument.
Following lateral mobilization, the surgeon uses a monopolar scissor to score the ileal mesentery, creating visible incisions along the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon uses the underwrist vessel sealer to efficiently harvest the ileal vessels, progressing down to the level of the cecum.

The surgeon elevates the right colic pedicle using a fenestrated bipolar grasper while scoring and developing a dissection plane between the retroperitoneal and peritoneal structures with a monopolar scissor. The ureter is visually identified and carefully preserved during the dissection.

The surgeon confirms the location of the right ureter and proceeds with dissection up the right gutter along the white line of TOLT towards the hepatic flexure. Fine dissection with monopolar scissors is used for skeletonization and high ligation of the right colic pedicle, which is achieved using the underwrist vessel sealer.
The surgeon identifies the avascular attachments between the mesocolon and duodenum, preparing to separate these structures.
The surgeon separates the omentum from the transverse colon along embryologic planes, revealing the lesser sac.

Enlarged lymph nodes are visible in the transverse mesocolon under magnification. The surgeon uses the monopolar scissors and fenestrated bipolar grasper with precise wristed motion to dissect around these nodes.

The surgeon continues dissection along avascular planes, separating the transverse colon and mesocolon from the duodenum and pancreas. This maneuver facilitates the high ligation of the middle colic pedicle.

The surgeon separates the transverse colon from its attachments to the omentum, extending the dissection distally. The greater curvature of the stomach, transverse colon, and small intestine are identified to orient the dissection planes.
The surgical instruments are withdrawn, and the robotic system is repositioned for a second docking to continue the procedure.

An eight millimeter port is placed in the right upper quadrant, and the previous assistant port is converted to a second eight millimeter port. The right lower quadrant port is now used as an assistant port. The patient is rotated right side down, and the robotic patient cart is docked over the left upper quadrant of the abdomen.

The surgeon dissects the left colon from the retroperitoneum in a lateral to medial direction. The presence of significant tissue edema due to partial obstruction and colitis is visible, aiding in the identification of appropriate dissection planes.
The surgeon identifies the left colic pedicle and transects it using the endorhist vessel sealer, visibly separating the pedicle from surrounding tissues.

After completing the mobilization of the splenic flexure and left colon, the robotic instruments are removed, and the patient cart is undocked. The surgeon performs the operation through a fan and steel incision, eviscerating the entire colon for resection and creating a side-to-side anastomosis between the terminal ileum and sigmoid colon.

The patient's post-operative course was uneventful. An enhanced recovery protocol included preoperative tap block with liposomal bupivacaine, early ambulation, oral intake starting on postoperative day zero, IV acetaminophen, PO-alvimepan, and minimization of narcotic use. The patient was discharged home on postoperative day four.

Final pathology reviewed a moderate to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the cecum, T four and zero, and a well to moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right colon, T three and zero. Fifty-six negative lymph nodes were in the specimen. All margins were negative. This included a three centimeter circumferential margin at the abdominal wall. Multifocal low grade dysplasia was noted in the remainder of the specimen. No adjuvant therapy was recommended.
At six months follow up, the patient was doing well with no evidence of disease.
